disease.name: Wilson Disease
disease.note: Autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism caused by ATP7B gene mutations. Impaired biliary copper excretion leads to toxic accumulation in liver, brain, cornea, and other organs. Highly treatable with chelation or zinc therapy; fatal if untreated.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Fulminant -> EMERGENCY transplant evaluation; Decompensated cirrhosis or severe neuro -> URGENT chelation; Asymptomatic/mild -> OUTPATIENT treatment initiation

historyFeatures.note: Copper deposition in Descemet's membrane; visible on slit lamp

examFeatures.note: Golden-brown rings at corneal limbus; pathognomonic; seen on slit lamp examination

bedside.name: Slit lamp eye examination
bedside.indication: detect Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper in Descemet's membrane); present in 95% of neurologic, 50% of hepatic presentations

laboratory.name: Serum ceruloplasmin
laboratory.indication: low (<20mg/dL) in 85% of Wilson disease; acute phase reactant can be falsely normal

laboratory.name: 24-hour urinary copper
laboratory.indication: >40mcg/24h suggests Wilson; >100mcg/24h is diagnostic in symptomatic patients

laboratory.name: Serum total and free copper
laboratory.indication: total copper low (low ceruloplasmin-bound) but free copper elevated (>25mcg/dL)

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: pattern varies: acute hepatitis, chronic hepatitis, or cirrhosis. ALP often low in fulminant Wilson

laboratory.name: FBC with reticulocyte count
laboratory.indication: Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia (sudden drop in Hb with elevated reticulocytes) is hallmark of acute decompensation

laboratory.name: ATP7B genetic testing
laboratory.indication: confirmatory; >800 mutations known; homozygosity or compound heterozygosity diagnostic

laboratory.name: Wiener/Leipzig diagnostic score
laboratory.indication: combined score: KF rings (2), neuropsychiatric symptoms (2), Coombs-neg hemolysis (1), ceruloplasmin low (2), 24h urine copper high (2), liver copper >250mcg/g (1), ATP7B mutations (4). Score >=4: diagnosis established.

imaging.name: Transient elastography (FibroScan)
imaging.indication: fibrosis assessment in chronic Wilson disease

imaging.name: Brain MRI with T2/FLAIR and susceptibility-weighted imaging
imaging.indication: characteristic: hyperintensities in basal ganglia (putamen, globus pallidus, caudate), thalamus, brainstem. 'Face of giant panda' sign in midbrain. DWI/ADC can show cytotoxic edema in acute neuro deterioration

imaging.name: Liver biopsy with copper quantification
imaging.indication: definitive diagnosis if non-invasive tests equivocal. Hepatic copper >250mcg/g dry weight (normal <50). Histology: steatosis, glycogenated nuclei, NASH-like, fibrosis.

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Wilson Disease; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Wilson Disease

pathways.criteria: Diagnosed by family screening, no symptoms, normal LFTs, no cirrhosis
pathways.treatment: Zinc acetate 50mg PO TID (elemental zinc). Dosing: 25-75mg elemental zinc daily in children (weight-based). Monitor 24h urine copper (target <75mcg/24h on zinc) and neurologic exam annually. Zinc induces metallothionein in enterocytes, blocking dietary copper absorption. Consider trientine 20mg/kg/day PO divided BID if zinc not tolerated.

pathways.criteria: Chronic hepatitis, Child-Pugh A/B, no decompensation, +/- KF rings, +/- mild neuro symptoms
pathways.treatment: Trientine 20mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1500mg/day) OR D-penicillamine 20mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1500mg/day) + pyridoxine 25-50mg PO daily. D-penicillamine: more side effects (nephrotic syndrome, lupus, bone marrow suppression, Goodpasture's). Trientine: better tolerated. Start with low dose and titrate up (to avoid neurologic worsening). Monitor 24h urine copper on treatment: chelation target >200mcg/24h on chelation. Regular neurologic exams.

pathways.criteria: Rapid onset jaundice, Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, coagulopathy (INR >2), encephalopathy, acutely elevated bilirubin, low ALP, young age (10-30 years)
pathways.treatment: URGENT transplant center referral. Fulminant Wilson has virtually 100% mortality without transplant. Transplant is the ONLY treatment for this presentation. Score for transplant urgency: bilirubin + ALP ratio (if bilirubin/ALP >2 and INR >1.5, high mortality risk). Bridging: plasmapheresis or plasma exchange to remove free copper and hemolysis products. Albumin dialysis (MARS) may be helpful. Do NOT start chelation (may worsen neurologic status).

pathways.criteria: Child-Pugh C, ascites, variceal bleed, encephalopathy, chronic Wilson disease
pathways.treatment: Transplant evaluation. Chelation therapy may stabilize some patients but transplant may be required. Trientine (preferred over D-penicillamine in decompensated). Manage complications (diuretics, lactulose, variceal banding). If transplant candidate, continue chelation pre-transplant. Post-transplant: chelation can be stopped (new liver has normal ATP7B), though neurologic symptoms may persist.

pathways.criteria: Neurologic symptoms (tremor, dysarthria, dystonia) with or without liver involvement
pathways.treatment: Trientine or D-penicillamine. WARNING: rapid chelation can cause paradoxical neurologic worsening (10-25% of patients). Start with low dose and gradual titration. Consider zinc as first-line for purely neurologic presentations (slower onset, less risk of worsening). Combined zinc + trientine may be used. Therapy is lifelong. Consider antioxidant: vitamin E 400-800 IU/day as adjunct. Monitor with serial neurologic exams, UWDRS scoring, and brain MRI.

pathways.criteria: Stable on treatment for >1 year, normal LFTs, stable neurologic function
pathways.treatment: Lifelong maintenance: trientine 15-20mg/kg/day PO divided BID OR D-penicillamine 15-20mg/kg/day PO divided BID. Zinc maintenance: 50mg elemental zinc PO TID (adults). Monitor 24h urine copper q6-12 months. Monitor LFTs, INR, albumin q3-6 months. Monitor for drug side effects (CBC, urinalysis q1-3 months). Annual eye exam for KF rings (regression with treatment). Monitor adherence (pill counts, 24h urine copper on zinc should be <75; on chelation 200-500).

disease.emergencyPriority: high for fulminant/acute severe; medium for symptomatic; low for presymptomatic
